map.condition: Counting minimum tables from ER schema
Deciding if a relationship attribute moves to an entity
map.use: \text{M:N} \Rightarrow \text{attribute stays in relation table;} \quad 1:\text{N} \Rightarrow \text{attribute moves to N-side}
\text{FK added to N-side entity table (no separate relation table needed)}

map.condition: Checking physical vs logical data independence
Participation constraint: every entity must participate

formulas.name: Strong Entity
formulas.latex: \text{Strong Entity: has its own primary key (PK)}
formulas.plain: Strong Entity: exists independently; has its own primary key
formulas.whenToUse: When an entity can be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone

terms.means: primary key — unique identifier for entity instances

formulas.trap: Confusing a strong entity with a weak entity when the key attribute is inherited from the parent

formulas.name: Weak Entity
formulas.latex: \text{Weak Entity: identified by PK of owner} + \text{partial key (discriminator)}
formulas.plain: Weak Entity: no full PK of its own; identified via owner entity PK + discriminator
formulas.whenToUse: When an entity cannot be uniquely identified without referring to another (owner) entity

terms.means: partial key — distinguishes weak entity instances among those with same owner

formulas.trap: Weak entity always has TOTAL participation in its identifying relationship — forgetting this in diagrams

formulas.name: Attribute Classification
formulas.plain: Attributes: Simple (atomic), Composite (parts), Multi-valued (set), Derived (computed)
formulas.whenToUse: When determining how to represent attributes in ER notation or map them to tables

terms.means: attribute can hold multiple values; shown with double ellipse
computed from other attributes; shown with dashed ellipse

formulas.trap: Multi-valued attributes require a separate table in relational mapping; not a column in the entity table

formulas.name: Key Attribute
formulas.latex: \text{Key attribute: uniquely identifies entity instance} \Rightarrow \underline{\text{underlined in ER diagram}}
formulas.plain: Key attribute: underlined in ER diagram; uniquely identifies entity instances
formulas.whenToUse: When identifying the primary key of an entity in ER notation

terms.means: combination of attributes that together form the key

formulas.trap: A composite key in ER does not mean multiple single-attribute keys; all parts together form one key

formulas.name: 1:1 (One-to-One) Relationship
formulas.latex: 1:1 \Rightarrow \text{FK in either entity table (or merge both)}
formulas.plain: 1:1: one entity in A relates to at most one in B; FK can go on either side
formulas.whenToUse: When each entity instance on both sides relates to at most one on the other side

terms.means: foreign key added to one entity table

formulas.trap: For 1:1 with total participation on one side, always put FK on the total-participation side to avoid NULLs

formulas.name: 1:N (One-to-Many) Relationship
formulas.plain: 1:N: FK (referencing 1-side PK) is placed in the N-side entity's table; no separate relation table needed
formulas.whenToUse: When one entity on the 1-side relates to many entities on the N-side

terms.means: entity whose PK becomes the FK in N-side
entity that holds the FK column

formulas.trap: Attributes of the relationship move to the N-side table, NOT a separate relationship table

formulas.name: M:N (Many-to-Many) Relationship
formulas.latex: M:N \Rightarrow \text{Separate relation table with PKs of both entities as FKs}
formulas.plain: M:N: requires its own relation table; PKs of both participating entities become FKs (and together form the PK of the relation table)
formulas.whenToUse: When entity instances on both sides can relate to many on the other side

terms.means: composite key: (PK_E1, PK_E2) plus any relationship attributes

formulas.trap: Relationship attributes of an M:N relationship stay in the relation table, not in either entity table

formulas.name: Ternary Relationship
formulas.plain: Ternary relationship: involves three entity sets; mapped to one relation table containing FKs of all three entities
formulas.whenToUse: When a relationship inherently involves three entity sets simultaneously

terms.means: degree-3 relationship — cannot always be decomposed into binary ones without loss

formulas.trap: A ternary relationship is NOT always equivalent to three binary relationships; decomposition may lose information

formulas.name: Total Participation (Existence Dependency)
formulas.latex: \text{Total participation} \Leftrightarrow \text{double line in ER; every entity must participate}
formulas.plain: Total participation: every instance of the entity MUST participate in the relationship (double line in ER)
formulas.whenToUse: When every entity instance is required to have at least one association in the relationship

terms.means: ER notation for total participation constraint

formulas.trap: Weak entities always have total participation in their identifying relationship — often tested

formulas.name: Partial Participation
formulas.latex: \text{Partial participation} \Leftrightarrow \text{single line in ER; entity may or may not participate}
formulas.plain: Partial participation: entity instances may optionally participate in the relationship (single line in ER)
formulas.whenToUse: When it is optional for an entity to have an association in the relationship

terms.means: ER notation for partial (optional) participation

formulas.trap: Partial participation maps to nullable FK or optional rows; do not confuse with cardinality notation

formulas.name: Min-Max (Structural) Constraint
formulas.plain: (min, max): min = minimum participation, max = maximum participation; (1,1) means total + at most one; (0,N) means optional + many
formulas.whenToUse: When expressing both lower and upper bounds on participation in a relationship

terms.means: partial participation (optional)
total participation (mandatory)
at most one (one-side)
many (many-side)

formulas.trap: In (min,max) notation the cardinality direction is sometimes reversed compared to crow's-foot; read from entity toward relationship

formulas.name: Minimum Table Count from ER Schema
formulas.plain: Minimum tables = (number of strong entities) + (number of weak entities) + (number of M:N relationships)
formulas.whenToUse: When GATE asks how many tables are needed to represent an ER schema in the relational model

terms.means: count of strong entity sets
count of weak entity sets
count of many-to-many relationship sets

formulas.trap: 1:1 and 1:N relationships do NOT add extra tables (FK is merged into entity tables); only M:N adds a table

formulas.name: Weak Entity Table Mapping
formulas.plain: Weak entity table: PK = (owner PK columns) + (discriminator attribute); always includes owner PK as FK
formulas.whenToUse: When converting a weak entity and its identifying relationship to a relational table

terms.means: primary key columns of the strong (owner) entity
partial key of the weak entity

formulas.trap: The identifying relationship itself does NOT produce a separate table; it is absorbed into the weak entity table

formulas.name: Multi-valued Attribute Mapping
formulas.plain: Multi-valued attribute maps to a separate table with columns: (entity PK as FK, attribute value); PK = both columns
formulas.whenToUse: When an entity has a multi-valued attribute (e.g., phone numbers, hobbies)

terms.means: primary key of the owning entity (becomes FK in new table)

formulas.trap: Storing multi-valued attributes as a comma-separated column violates 1NF; a separate table is required

formulas.name: Relationship Attribute Placement
formulas.latex: 1:N \Rightarrow \text{attr} \to N\text{-side table}; \quad M:N \Rightarrow \text{attr stays in relation table}
formulas.plain: 1:N relationship attributes move to N-side entity table. M:N relationship attributes stay in the separate relation table.
formulas.whenToUse: When deciding where to place attributes that belong to a relationship

terms.means: attribute describing the association, not a single entity

formulas.trap: For 1:1 with total participation, relationship attributes should go with the total-participation side entity

formulas.name: Specialisation (Top-Down)
formulas.plain: Specialisation: top-down; superclass is refined into subclasses sharing common attributes. ISA hierarchy.
formulas.whenToUse: When a high-level entity is broken down into more specific sub-entities

terms.means: is-a relationship between subclass and superclass
generalised entity at the top

formulas.trap: Disjoint vs. overlapping: in disjoint, an instance belongs to at most one subclass; in overlapping, it may belong to multiple

formulas.name: Generalisation (Bottom-Up)
formulas.plain: Generalisation: bottom-up; common attributes of subclasses are factored into a new superclass
formulas.whenToUse: When multiple entity sets share attributes and are abstracted into a common superclass

terms.means: reverse of specialisation — unifying entities upward

formulas.trap: Generalisation and specialisation produce the same ER structure; the difference is only in the design direction

formulas.name: Aggregation
formulas.latex: \text{Aggregation: treat a relationship as an entity to form a higher-level relationship}
formulas.plain: Aggregation: groups an entity + relationship into a composite 'super-entity' so it can participate in another relationship; used when a relationship itself needs to relate to another entity
formulas.whenToUse: When a relationship set must participate in another relationship set (relationship of relationships)

terms.means: a relationship treated as an abstract entity

formulas.trap: Aggregation is used to avoid ternary relationships when the higher-level relationship only involves one of the original entities and the aggregate

formulas.name: Data Independence (Physical vs Logical)
formulas.latex: \text{Physical independence} > \text{Logical independence} \;(\text{physical is easier to achieve})
formulas.plain: Physical data independence (changing storage/access methods) is easier to achieve than logical data independence (changing schema without affecting applications)
formulas.whenToUse: When answering True/False questions about which type of data independence is easier

terms.means: ability to change physical storage without changing logical schema
ability to change logical schema without changing application programs

formulas.trap: GATE classic: 'Logical data independence is easier than physical' is FALSE; physical is easier

formulas.name: Three-Schema Architecture (ANSI/SPARC)
formulas.plain: Three levels: External (user views), Conceptual (logical structure / ER model), Internal (physical storage). Mappings between levels enable data independence.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about DBMS architecture, levels of abstraction, or data independence mechanisms

terms.means: user views / subschemas
community view; ER or relational schema
physical storage schema

formulas.trap: The ER model sits at the Conceptual level, not the External level

formulas.name: ER Diagram Notation Summary
formulas.plain: Rectangle: entity. Double rectangle: weak entity. Diamond: relationship. Double diamond: identifying relationship. Ellipse: attribute. Double ellipse: multi-valued. Dashed ellipse: derived. Underline: key attribute.
formulas.whenToUse: When interpreting or drawing an ER diagram in GATE questions

terms.means: weak entity set
identifying (weak) relationship
multi-valued attribute

formulas.trap: The identifying relationship (double diamond) is always between the weak entity and its owner — not any other relationship
